coredata.dc:title: Environmentally benign conversion of waste polyethylene terephthalate to fluorescent carbon dots for “on-off-on” sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yaoping
Gao, Zhijin
Yang, Jie
Chen, Hui
Han, Lei
coredata.dc:description: The increasing production of waste polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is a growing problem worldwide. Environmentally benign conversion of waste PET to valuable materials remains a substantial challenge. In this paper, we developed a green route to reuse waste PET for low-cost synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via air oxidation followed by hydrothermal treatment in aqueous H2O2 solution. No expensive, corrosive, or toxic reagents, or severe conditions were required in the synthetic process. The resultant CDs possessed abundant oxygenous groups and unique photoluminescence (PL) properties, which showed a highly selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) through a PL quenching effect (on-off). The fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+ could be restored specifically with pyrophosphate anion (PPi), rendering the CDs/Fe3+ sensor promising for PPi detection (off-on). The linear ranges for Fe3+ and PPi detections were 0.5–400 and 2–600 μM, and the limit of detections (LODs) were 0.21 and 0.86 μM, respectively. The sensing system applied for Fe3+ and PPi assays in real water samples and human urine achieved good results. After detailed investigations, a possible electron transfer process was proposed for explaining the “on-off-on” sensing mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Environmentally benign conversion
Waste polyethylene terephthalate
Carbon dots
Sensing
Ferric and pyrophosphate ions

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and PPi

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City ( 2017A610230 ), the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University , and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21471086 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectrum of CDs. Inset in (a): high-resolution TEM image of CDs.
(a) XPS survey, (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) FTIR spectra of CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of CDs. Insets in (a): photographs of the CD solution excited by daylight and a UV lamp (365 nm). Inset in (b): normalized PL spectra of CDs.

(a) PL spectra of CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.5, 20, 40, 80, 120, 180, 240, 320, 400, 600, and 800 μM). Inset in (a): a nice linear relationship between the quenching ratio (I 0 − I)/I 0 and the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.5–400 μM. (b) Selectivity of CDs toward Fe3+. All the concentrations of the metal ions were 400 μM. (I and I 0 referred to the PL intensities of CDs at 434 nm with and without Fe3+, respectively. C CDs = 5 mg L−1, λex = 340 nm.)

(a) PL spectra of CDs/Fe3+ upon addition of various concentrations of PPi (from bottom to top: 0, 2, 40, 100, 160, 240, 320, 400, 500, 600, 800, and 1000 μM). Inset in (a): a nice linear relationship between (I − I 0)/I 0 and the PPi concentration in the range of 2–600 μM. (b) Selectivity of CDs/Fe3+ toward PPi. All the concentrations of the anions were 600 μM. (I and I 0 referred to the PL intensities of CDs/Fe3+ at 434 nm with and without PPi, respectively. C CDs = 5 mg L−1, C Fe 3+ = 400 μM, λex = 340 nm.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs/Fe3+, CDs/Fe3+/PPi, Fe3+, and Fe3+/PPi (C CDs = 5 mg L−1, C Fe 3+ = 400 μM, C PPi = 1000 μM). The theoretical curves were obtained by the physical addition of the spectra of the CD solution and the Fe3+ solution (dotted line, blue color), and the spectra of the CD solution and the Fe3+/PPi solution (dotted line, yellow color). (b) PL decay profiles of CDs, CDs/Fe3+, and CDs/Fe3+/PPi (λex = 340 nm, λem = 434 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A diagram about the synthesis of CDs from waste plastic bottle made of PET for “on-off-on” sensing of Fe3+ and PPi.
Results of Fe3+ detection in real water samples using the CD sensor.
Results of PPi detection in human urine using the CDs/Fe3+ sensing system.

head.ce:title: Environmentally benign conversion of waste polyethylene terephthalate to fluorescent carbon dots for “on-off-on” sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The reuse of waste polyethylene terephthalate for green and low-cost synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for “on-off-on” sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions.

The increasing production of waste polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is a growing problem worldwide. Environmentally benign conversion of waste PET to valuable materials remains a substantial challenge. In this paper, we developed a green route to reuse waste PET for low-cost synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via air oxidation followed by hydrothermal treatment in aqueous H2O2 solution. No expensive, corrosive, or toxic reagents, or severe conditions were required in the synthetic process. The resultant CDs possessed abundant oxygenous groups and unique photoluminescence (PL) properties, which showed a highly selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion (Fe3+) through a PL quenching effect (on-off). The fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+ could be restored specifically with pyrophosphate anion (PPi), rendering the CDs/Fe3+ sensor promising for PPi detection (off-on). The linear ranges for Fe3+ and PPi detections were 0.5–400 and 2–600 μM, and the limit of detections (LODs) were 0.21 and 0.86 μM, respectively. The sensing system applied for Fe3+ and PPi assays in real water samples and human urine achieved good results. After detailed investigations, a possible electron transfer process was proposed for explaining the “on-off-on” sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing usage of polyethylene terephthalate (PET), a favorable packaging polymer for beverages, has resulted in the production of huge quantities of solid wastes [1]. These PET wastes are non-degradable and environmental hazard, that should be properly disposed of. Generally, PET wastes are incinerated to provide energy, or recycled to make polyester fibers, strapping, and non-food containers [2]. Recently, alternative routes have been proposed to process PET wastes as promising feedstocks for low-cost synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes and graphene [3,4]. Besides these nanomaterials, can PET wastes be converted into other high-valued products? The answer is absolutely yes.

Carbon dots (CDs) with lateral sizes below 10 nm represent a fascinating class of nanocarbons, showing multiple applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, optoelectronics and photocatalysis [5–11], because of their outstanding optical properties, low environmental hazard, excellent biocompatibility and high chemical stability [12–15]. Over the past several years, various precursors have been employed to prepare CDs with diverse synthetic strategies. For the purpose of reducing production cost and saving resources, conversion of wastes such as used frying oil and candle combustion soot into CDs is preferable [16,17]. Unfortunately, highly corrosive sulfuric and nitric acid are required in these cases, which are harmful to the environment. Very recently, waste expanded polystyrene has been used to fabricate CDs by a solvothermal method [18]. However, chloroform, a volatile and somewhat toxic organic solvent, is necessary in this reaction, that raises the environmental concerns. Up to now, green synthesis of CDs from waste resources is still a great challenge.

Ferric ion (Fe3+) plays an essential role in a variety of biological processes, including enzyme catalysis, oxygen transportation, and electron transfer [19]. Abnormal Fe3+ levels in human body can induce physiological damages like liver injury, renal failure, heart disease, etc [20]. Pyrophosphate anion (PPi or P2O7 4−) is also vital in biological systems because it is closely related to DNA polymerization, cellular metabolism and enzymatic reactions [21]. Both deficiency and excessive accumulation of PPi in living cells can lead to some disorders such as arthritis and chondrocalcinosis [22]. At present, the detections of Fe3+ and PPi, however, usually require sophisticated instruments and tedious methods. Thus, it is urgent and important to develop a facile way to determinate the amounts of Fe3+ and PPi in biological and environmental samples.

Herein, for the first time, we demonstrate the reuse of waste plastic bottles made of PET as a carbon source for environmentally friendly synthesis of fluorescent CDs via air oxidation followed by hydrothermal treatment in aqueous H2O2 solution. Compared with previously reported methods, our approach using waste PET resource was more economic and much greener, without need of any expensive, corrosive, or toxic reagent, or severe synthetic condition. Interestingly, it was found that the resulting CDs with abundant oxygenous functional groups could serve as a highly selective and sensitive nanosensor for “on-off-on” detection of Fe3+ and PPi even in complex environmental and biological conditions (Scheme 1 ). Moreover, after detailed investigations, a possible electron transfer process was proposed to elucidate the sensing mechanism.

Plastic bottles containing spring water were obtained from Nongfu Spring Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). After drinking, the waste bottles made of PET were reused as raw materials for synthesis of CDs. Hydrogen peroxide (30 wt%), Iron (III) chloride, and sodium pyrophosphate decahydrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

CDs were fabricated by air oxidation of waste PET followed by hydrothermal treatment in aqueous H2O2 solution. In a typical procedure, waste PET was put into an open ceramic crucible and heated at 350 °C for 2 h in air to form a dark brown oxidized product. Then, 0.5 g of the product was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing 30 mL of H2O2 solution in a concentration of 4 wt%. After hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 12 h, a brown dispersion of CDs was got. The CD dispersion was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove the residue. Further purification of CDs was conducted through a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut off: 1000 Da) for 3 days in the dark (changed deionized water every 8 h). After freeze-drying, about 15 mg of the CD powder was finally obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of Fe3+, an aqueous FeCl3 solution (1980 μL) with a certain concentration was mixed with a dispersion of CDs (20 μL, 0.5 mg mL−1). The PL spectrum with excitation wavelength of 340 nm was recorded after the mixture was equilibrated for 2 min. In the selectivity experiment for Fe3+ sensing, the aqueous FeCl3 solution (1980 μL, 404 μM) was replaced by KCl, AgNO3, Ca(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Hg(ClO4)2, Mg(NO3)2, Mn(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3, and Al(NO3)3. For Fe3+ assays in real samples, the local lake water (Sun Lake, Ningbo, China), river water (Yong River, Ningbo, China), and tap water were collected and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min following by being filtered through a 0.22 membrane. After that, the real water sample (1780 μL) was mixed with the aqueous FeCl3 solution (200 μL) with a certain concentration and a dispersion of CDs (20 μL, 0.5 mg mL−1). Then, the fluorescence measurement was performed as described above. All the tests were conducted in triplicate.

For detection of PPi, an aqueous Na4P2O7 solution (1980 μL) with a certain concentration was mixed with a dispersion of CDs (20 μL, 0.5 mg mL−1) containing FeCl3 (40 mM). After 2 min, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. To investigate the interference of other anions, the aqueous Na4P2O7 solution (1980 μL, 606 μM) was replaced by NaF, NaCl, NaBr, NaI, NaNO3, Na2S, Na2BrO3, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, Na2S2O3, Na2S2O8, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaH2PO4, and Na2HPO4. The human urine sample was pretreated before use [23]. Typically, an aqueous HCl solution (0.1 mL, 6 M) was added to the urine sample (100 mL), and then the mixture was purified by multi-walled carbon nanotubes (0.2 g). Next, the solutions of HClO4 (1.6 mL, 1 M), ammonium molybdate (0.4 mL, 0.08 M), and triethylamine hydrochloride (0.8 mL, 0.1 M, pH 5.0) were successively added to the purified sample (7 mL). A clear supernatant was obtained by twice centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 20 min) of the mixture. After that, the Mg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions in urine were precipitated by NaOH. Finally, the pH of the supernatant was adjusted to 7.0 with HCl. For PPi assay in human urine, the pretreated urine sample (1780 μL) was mixed with the aqueous Na4P2O7 solution (200 μL) with a certain concentration and a dispersion of CDs (20 μL, 0.5 mg mL−1) containing FeCl3 (40 mM). All other procedures were the same as above.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ká radiation (1.54056 Å). Raman spectrum of the as-prepared sample was obtained on a Renishaw inVia-reflex spectroscopy with a laser at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with an AXIS UL TRA DLD spectrograph with Al/Kα as the source. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum was collected in the KBr medium on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were monitored by a UV-3101PC spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. Zeta potential measurements were done on a nano Z zeta potential analyzer. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were measured with a Horiba FL3-111 spectroscope. The quantification of Fe3+ in real water sample was performed on a contrAA 700 atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS).

PET, a kind of thermoplastic polyester, has high thermal stability and is resistant to mineral acids, oxidizing agents, sunlight and microorganisms under ambient conditions [24]. With the purpose of environmentally friendly conversion of the “inert” PET wastes to fluorescent CDs, a two-step synthetic strategy involving air thermal oxidation and H2O2-assisted hydrothermal technique was designed. Open-air treatment of waste PET at 350 °C for 2 h induced cross-links and discoloration of the polymer [25], resulting in a dark brown oxidized product. Such an oxidized product with various chromophoric systems and plentiful oxygen-containing groups was much more reactive than the initial PET. It could turn into CDs via hydrothermal treatment in aqueous H2O2 solution at 180 °C.

In order to obtain the desired CDs, the synthetic conditions were optimized. In the first step, the reaction temperature was a vital parameter. Lower temperature, for example 300 °C, was not powerful enough for completely destroying the robust structure of PET. The insufficiently oxidized product of PET could not effectively transform into CDs by hydrothermal treatment even in high-concentration H2O2 solutions (10–15 wt%) at 180 °C. Higher temperature, for instance 400 °C, led to the serious degradation of PET, producing a large amount of acetaldehyde [25,26], and thus reducing the yield of the wanted oxidized product of PET. Therefore, a moderate temperature, 350 °C, was appropriate. In the second step, the H2O2 concentration was a critical factor. When the H2O2 concentration raised from 0.0 to 4.0 wt% (0.5 g of the oxidized product of PET was used), the color of the resultant CD solution turned from light yellow to red brown, indicating the yield of CDs increased. However, further increasing the H2O2 concentration from 4.0 to 10.0 wt% caused the color change of the CD solution from red brown to light yellow to colorless. It was proposed that excessive amount of H2O2 resulted in over-oxidation of the carbon precursor, generating the gas of CO2, and ultimately lowering the yield of CDs. Consequently, the H2O2 concentration of 4 wt% was selected.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 a) showed that the resultant CDs had a nearly circular shape and were well dispersed. Their diameters ranged from 3 to 10 nm with a mean size of approximately 6 nm by counting more than 150 nanoparticles (Fig. 1b). Clear Lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm were observed in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1a, inset), which were corresponding to the (1 0 0) in-plane lattice of graphite, indicating the good crystallinity of CDs [13,27]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1c) exhibited a broad peak at 2θ angle of 24.2°. The calculated interlayer spacing was 0.37 nm, larger than the (0 0 2) spacing of graphite (0.34 nm). The small difference in interlayer spacing between CDs and graphite was ascribed to the presence of abundant functional groups at the edges of CDs that enlarged the spacing of graphene layers [10]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1d) displayed the characteristics of D and G bands at 1343 and 1586 cm−1 respectively. The D band reflected the disorder-induced sp3 carbon atoms, while the G band represented the E2g vibration mode of sp2 bonded carbon. The relative intensity of the characteristic bands (I D/I G) was 0.63, suggesting that CDs contained both sp2 carbon clusters and sp3 defects, possessing a partially disordered graphite structure [28,29].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out to determine the elemental composition and chemical bonding of the sample. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) showed pronounced signals of C1s at ca. 284 eV and O1s at ca. 532 eV with atomic ratio of 67.5% and 32.5% respectively, revealing that carbon and oxygen were the key elements of CDs. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) could be resolved into three peaks of CC/CC at 284.5 eV, CO at 286.1 eV, and CO at 288.7 eV [30]. The large peak of CC/CC demonstrated that the CC component is the main carbon bonding configuration and CDs had a graphitic matrix structure. The small peaks of CO and CO suggested that CDs were functionalized with plentiful oxygenous groups on the surface, like hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibited two oxygen states of CO at 531.7 eV and CO at 533.0 eV [8], further confirmed the presence of oxygenous groups. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 2d) presented the characteristic absorption bands of OH stretching vibration at 3440 cm−1, CH stretching vibration at 2925 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1712 cm−1, CC stretching vibration at 1610 cm−1, CH bending vibration at 1409 cm−1, and CO stretching vibration at 1251 cm−1. These results were consistent with the XPS analysis. By combining all the characterization data, it was concluded that the as-synthesized materials were carbon-rich, nanosized dots, which had graphite-like structure and bore abundant oxygenous groups on the surface.

We investigated the optical properties of CDs. The aqueous solution of CDs was transparent and exhibited a strong blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (Fig. 3 a, insets). As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs showed a broad band around 270 nm, which was associated with the n-π* transition of CO groups [31]. The PL spectra revealed an excitation-dependent emission behavior of CDs (Fig. 3b). The maximum PL peak was centered at 434 nm under 340 nm excitation. The PL peak gradually moved from 434 to 532 nm with decrease of intensity when the excitation wavelength shifted from 340 to 500 nm. Such a PL feature probably originated from the diverse surface emissive traps and different particle sizes of CDs [32]. The quantum yield was measured to be 5.2% by using quinine sulfate as a standard (the calculation method was described in the supplementary material), which was higher than those of waste-derived CDs [16,17,19]. The PL intensity of CDs remained nearly constant at different pH values (Fig. S1), in high NaCl concentration media (Fig. S2), under continuous UV exposure (Fig. S3), and after a long-time storage in air (Fig. S4), indicating the good PL stability, which offered a great advantage for their further applications.

The unique surface property and interesting PL behavior of CDs promoted us to explore their sensing applications. It was found that Fe3+, one of the most important metal ion for organisms, could effectively quench the PL of CDs. Fig. 4 a recorded a gradual decrease of PL intensity upon addition of increased concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 800 μM. A nice linear relationship between the quenching ratio (I 0 − I)/I 0 and the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.5–400 μM was observed (Fig. 4a, inset). The linear regression equation could be described as (I 0 − I)/I 0 = 0.00141 × C Fe 3+ (μM) + 0.00182 with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.21 μM according to the 3σ rule, lower than the maximum level of Fe3+ (0.3 mg L−1, equivalent to 5.4 μM) permitted in drinking water by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [34]. Other metal ions, including K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, and Al3+, had no significant PL quenching effect, rather different from Fe3+ under the same conditions (Fig. 4b), demonstrating the remarkable selectivity of the CD sensing platform toward Fe3+. In comparison to the former fluorescence approaches for Fe3+ detection (Table S1) [20,21,33–36], our method showed comparable results and had the advantages of simplicity and cheapness.

We evaluated the applicability of CDs for detecting Fe3+ in real samples collected from lake water, river water, and tap water. The real aqueous samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min following by being filtered through a 0.22 membrane. The samples were then spiked with Fe3+ at different concentration levels. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of Fe3+ in real samples were in the range of 93.2–109.2%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 4.3%, both of which were suggestive of the high accuracy and credibility of the fluorescence analysis. For comparison, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) method was used for assay of Fe3+ in the same samples (Table S2). It clearly revealed that the Fe3+ concentrations determined by our fluorescence method were well consistent with the results obtained by AAS, which further proved that the CD-based sensing system for Fe3+ detection was quite reliable.

Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs quenched by Fe3+ could be easily recovered by adding PPi into the CDs/Fe3+ solution (Fig. 5 a). The fluorescence recovery ratio reached up to 95.1% upon addition of PPi at the concentration of 1000 μM. As seen in the inset of Fig. 5a, the change of PL intensity (I − I 0)/I 0 was almost proportional to the PPi concentration ranging from 2 to 600 μM, and the calibration curve could be expressed as (I − I 0)/I 0 = 0.00142 × C PPi (μM) + 0.01145 with an R 2 of 0.998. The LOD was calculated to be 0.86 μM, which was lower than the recently reported values [21,37–39]. The CDs/Fe3+ sensing system performed high selectivity toward PPi. The additions of various other anions and biomolecules, including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, S2−, BrO3 2−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, cysteine, deoxyribose, ribose, lactic acid, folic acid, uric acid, and cholesterol, could not cause any apparent fluorescence enhancement, that were in stark contrast to PPi (Figs. 5b and S5). Fig. S6 showed that the CD-based sensor could be repeatedly used for detection of Fe3+ and PPi. Compared with the previous methods for PPi detection (Table S3), the proposed strategy herein exhibited excellent performance in terms of high sensitivity, low detection limit, good selectivity, wide linear range, and low cost of material preparation.

The CDs/Fe3+ sensing system was applied for determination of PPi in human urine according to the procedures described in the experimental section. The results were listed in Table 2 . Using the standard addition method, the recoveries of spiked PPi ranged from 96.4% to 105.0% with RSDs lower than 5.7%, which revealed that the developed fluorescence sensor had a great potential for PPi detection in real biological samples.

It has been reported that Fe3+ can coordinate the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching [40–42]. Compared with other metal ions, the binding ability between the phenolic hydroxyl groups and Fe3+ is much stronger, so Fe3+ can be specifically identified [42]. To investigate the sensing mechanism, detailed experiments were carried out. The Stern-Volmer equation was measured to determine the quenching constant, and a value of 2.5 × 103 mol−1 (298 K) was obtained (Fig. S7). The quenching constant was large, suggesting the static quenching mechanism, which refers to the formation of nonfluorescent complexes by combining ground-state fluorescence materials with quencher molecules [20]. At evaluated temperature of 318 K, the quenching constant was nearly unchanged (2.4 × 103 mol−1), revealing that Fe3+ did not dissociate from CDs even at 318 K, which was also an evidence of static quenching mechanism. The FTIR analysis (Fig. S8) showed that the peak areas of CO and CO reduced sharply after addition of Fe3+, indicating that Fe3+ indeed complexed with the oxygenous groups of CDs. The XPS O1s spectra (Fig. S9) further proved the complexation between Fe3+ and OH groups of CDs, because the signal of FeO bond appeared upon Fe3+ introduction [42]. Fig. 6 a revealed that in the fluorescence quenching process, the experimental curve (the absorption spectrum of CDs/Fe3+) did not coincide with the theoretical curve (the physical addition of the spectra of the CD solution and the Fe3+ solution), indicating that Fe3+ could interact with the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs to form CD-Fe3+ complexes, and the excited electrons would transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+, resulting in PL quenching via the nonradiative electron-hole annihilation [43,44]; while in the fluorescence recovery process, the experimental curve (the absorption spectrum of CDs/Fe3+/PPi) matched well with the theoretical curve (the physical addition of the spectra of the CD solution and the Fe3+/PPi solution), implying that compared with CDs, PPi possessed a stronger affinity toward Fe3+ to generate PPi-Fe3+ complexes, thus releasing CDs and bringing about PL recovery [21,45]. The zeta potential of CDs shifted from −14.6 to 3.7 mV in the presence of Fe3+, whereas the value returned to −13.9 mV after further addition of PPi (Fig. S10), which supported that Fe3+ initially coordinated with the phenolic hydroxyl groups of CDs and then detached from CDs to bind with PPi upon its introduction [23]. Fig. 6b showed that the average PL lifetimes of CDs, CDs/Fe3+, and CDs/Fe3+/PPi were nearly identical (2.39, 2.10, and 2.28, respectively), confirming the static quenching mechanism of CDs caused by Fe3+ due to the formation of nonfluorescent CD-Fe3+ complexes [20,46].

In summary, an environmentally friendly method was developed for conversion of waste PET to fluorescent CDs via air oxidation followed by H2O2-assisted hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared CDs had plentiful oxygenous groups and distinctive PL properties. The fluorescence of CDs could be specifically quenched by Fe3+, and then selectively recovered upon addition of PPi. Based on these observations, a sensing system was set up for “on-off” detection of Fe3+ and “off-on” determination of PPi with LODs of 0.21 and 0.86 μM respectively. The accurate assays of Fe3+ and PPi in real water samples and human urine were successfully achieved. With deep investigations of the sensing mechanism, we proposed that the electron transfer between Fe3+ and CDs accounted for the PL quenching and recovery processes during Fe3+ and PPi detections. Our work provides an environmentally benign route to reuse waste PET for synthesis of CDs and extends the practical applications of CDs in sensing field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2017A610230), the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University, and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21471086).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.12.016.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Twenty years of PET bottle to bottle recycling—an overview
PET containers in Brazil: opportunities and challenges of a logistics model for post-consumer waste recycling
Synthesis of branched, nano channeled, ultrafine and nano carbon tubes from PET wastes using the arc discharge method
Green synthesis of graphene from recycled PET bottle wastes for use in the adsorption of dyes in aqueous solution
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging
Ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide solution: hydrothermal synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots as multifunctional nanosensors
Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon dots—Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics
Recent advances in bioapplications of C-dots
Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Green synthesis of multifunctionalized, nitrogen-doped, highly fluorescent carbon dots from waste expanded polystyrene and its application in the fluorimetric detection of Au3+ ions in aqueous media
Green and size-controllable synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles from waste plastic bags
